metadata.title: The Window That Closes: How Spotify's Algorithm Decides a Song's Fate in Two Weeks

beats.narration_text: Talofa — this is Liam, in for Bear. Based on the essay 'The Window That Closes' by Nik Bear Brown, for NortheasternISE / Humanitarians AI. Why does Spotify care about who saves your song? Because the first two to four weeks after release shape algorithmic routing for the track's entire lifecycle. A 500,000-follower playlist with 2% saves from an incoherent audience is categorically worse than a 5,000-follower playlist with 15% saves from a coherent taste community. Scale is not the variable. Signal quality is.

props.segment: The Window That Closes: How Spotify's Algorithm Decides a Song's Fate in Two Weeks
props.command: Why does Spotify care about who saves your song?
props.runningText: reading Spotify algorithmic routing and contamination window analysis…
output: Contamination window: first 2-4 weeks after release shape lifecycle routing.

beats.act: two release trajectories — coherent vs incoherent
beats.narration_text: Two tracks released the same week. Track A is placed on a 500,000-follower playlist spanning hip hop, country, and ambient electronica — a broad audience with low taste coherence. Saves in week one: 2%, which sounds good but is 2% of people who have no particular affinity for this style of music. The algorithm reads this as a weak signal — it cannot determine what kind of listener saves this track. Track B is placed on a 5,000-follower playlist of dedicated listeners for exactly this genre. Saves in week one: 15%. The algorithm reads a strong, coherent signal — it knows exactly what kind of listener responds to this track. At week eight, Track A has been routed to nobody new. Track B is in 40,000 Discover Weekly playlists.

manim.description: Two 8-week trajectory lines from release. Track A (500K playlist, 2% saves): briefly spikes then falls flat, zero Discover Weekly routing. Track B (5K playlist, 15% saves): lower initial numbers but Discover Weekly routing kicks in at week 3-4, ends at 40K impressions. Algorithmic routing event annotated.

beats.act: save rate x audience coherence routing matrix
beats.narration_text: The routing outcome is a function of two variables: save rate and audience coherence. High save rate from an incoherent audience: the algorithm cannot build a taste cluster from the data. It does not route. Low save rate from a coherent audience: insufficient signal strength. No routing. High save rate from a coherent audience: the algorithm has a clear picture of the listener who saves this track. It can find more of them. Routing begins. The contamination window is the period during which the algorithm is building its model of the track. Fragmented behavioral data during that window produces no coherent taste cluster, and the window closes.

animated_deck.description: 2x2 matrix: axes are save rate (low/high) and audience coherence (low/high). Four quadrants. High rate + low coherence: 'no routing — fragmented signal'. Low rate + high coherence: 'no routing — insufficient signal'. High rate + high coherence: 'routing begins — taste cluster found'. Fourth quadrant: 'minimum viable' zone.

beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. The contamination window is two to four weeks after release. Fragmented behavioral data during that window produces no coherent taste cluster — the algorithm stops routing. 500,000 followers, 2% saves: zero Discover Weekly. 5,000 followers, 15% saves: 40,000 Discover Weekly impressions. Scale is not the variable. Signal quality is. The window closes. After it closes, the routing decision is made and the model is largely set for the track's lifecycle.

props.artifactHeading: Algorithmic routing in two to four weeks
artifactLines: Contamination window: first 2-4 weeks shape lifecycle routing.
500K followers / 2% saves → fragmented signal → zero Discover Weekly.
5K followers / 15% saves → coherent cluster → 40K Discover Weekly impressions.
Scale is not the variable. Signal quality is.

beats.narration_text: Your turn. Here is the prompt — read it with me. 'Analyze the listening data for a track you've released or can access. What behavioral signals appear in the first two weeks? Do they predict long-term streaming velocity?' That prediction question is the test of the contamination window theory. Pull the first-two-week behavioral data — saves, skip rates, completion rates, playlist add rates — and check whether they correlate with streaming velocity at month three. If they do, you have found the signal the algorithm is reading. Liam, in for Bear.
